segments.text: Grant Kell on the, I'm chairing of this subcommittee today.
Uh, we've got fifteen minutes of your time. We have your submission.
Please take it as read, but feel free to highlight a few of the key points, and
we'd love a few ques- opportunity for some questions.
So the floor is yours.
Yeah. So, good morning, everybody, and thank you for the,
opportunity to speak to you in person today, on the
Natural, Environment and Planning Bills.
Uh, Warima, chair of Waikato Regional Council. So good morning.
Um, so the Waikato Regional Council overall supports the intention to
replace the RMA and to simplify the resource management
system. Uh, but it's something we need to get right.
So we need to have resource management legislation that is clear,
workable, and will withstand election cycles.
So that is a key for us. Um, I won't go over what you've probably heard from
others, but just want to focus in on what's important for the Waikato
region. Um, so Waikato region, we are twenty percent of the
land area of the North Island. Ten percent approximately of New
Zealand's, population live and work in our
region. We are ten percent approximately of the New Zealand
GDP, which makes us the fourth largest
economically producing region. Uh, we have fifteen
percent of the country's councils.
Uh, so that's twelve local government entities, either in the, the region
completely or a part of. Um, big part of our
region is our renewable energy resources.
Uh, the majority of Taupō's volcanic geothermal area
is, is in our region, as well as items like the Lake
Taupō, Waikato River, Hauraki Plains, significant
wetlands, coastal areas, including the Firth of Thames and Coromandel, which are
obviously part of the Hauraki Gulf Park.
Uh, we have forty iwi and, a hundred and eighty hapū in
our region. So we have fifteen years of co-governance, co-management with our
river iwi. Excuse me.
Uh, and it's a place that we are comfortable in, and we're national leaders in this
field. So we wish to see those deep relationships and foundation of
trust that we have built with iwi,
and have been the cornerstone of previous resource management system,
to go forward and actually strengthen in this new
system. We acknowledge that our sys- our, submission
is lengthy, and we hope that it has been viewed constructively.
So the main themes of our submissions are highlighted on paragraph
eight on pages two and three, and they're also further
elaborated on pages four through to fourteen with
accompanying detail. Uh, we have a couple of key matters that we would like
to draw to the subcommittee's attention.
Um, and one for me before I pass over to Chris McClay, our CE, is
drawing on my recent experiences sitting as a commissioner on our coastal
plan hearing. Uh, having the likes of Aquaculture New Zealand, seafood
industry, Royal Forest and Bird, and New Zealand Sports Fishing Council being
able to participate, in the process has added immense
value to that process. Our submission on paragraph
sixty-one and submission point one zero four asks that the
restriction to qualifying residents, is
kept in the consent process,
but removed from plan making, and that enables those sector
and interest groups to participate in that plan making
process. Uh, I'll now hand over to Chris, with some more key
matters for us. Thank you.
Yeah. Uh, thank you through the chair, Grant and, and committee members for this
opportunity. I've got two specialists with me, Tracy May and
Mark Brocklesby, in case there's any questions o-of a technical
nature. I'm gonna focus on four matters which are of
importance to the Waikato. Um, the first of these is the proposed
permitted activity system. I'd refer to paragraph sixty-five on page
thirteen of our submission, submission point seventy-six on page
eighteen, and submission point one forty-four on page
forty-eight. We do see some fish hooks in the new permitted
activity system that will make things prac-- challenging from a
practical point of view, particularly if we're trying to reduce
red tape. The provisions in effect make a pseudo consent process in our
mind that adds a lot of impost to what should be a simple
system. Presently drafted, the proposed permitted activity
system strongly resembles a consent process with registration
and monitoring requirements. Our council has had one of the most
permissive regional plans in operation in the country for the last twenty years or
so. We have focused on proactive monitoring of
high-risk activities rather than monitoring all activities
or requiring all activities to register.
Our suggested amendments allow for more discretion,
hoping to make things simpler to manage activities without additional
system cost to both authorities and
landowners. In other words, we think our amendment-- our, our
suggestions would make things simpler, faster, and cheaper
than, than is in the legislation.
The next matter I'd like to raise is to do with, Te Tiriti o Waitanga
or Te Awa o Waikato, which is more commonly known as the vision and
strategy for the Waikato and Waipā rivers, and
specifically the equivalent effect, that is,
introduced into the legislation.
I referred in our submission to paragraph E on page
four, paragraph twenty-eight on page
nine, submi-submission point eighty-three on page
twenty, submission point one zero two on page twenty-nine, and submission
point one seventy-one on page sixty-four.
Simply, Te Tiriti o Waitanga, the vision and strategy, is the
primary direction-setting document for the Waikato River and activities
within its catchment. Under iwi river settlement
legislation, where there's inconsistency
between Te Tiriti o Waitanga and any national direction, then
Te Tiriti o Waitanga prevails. Now, this is absolutely central to
understandUnder the Treaty Settlement legislation, if there's an
inconsistency between Te Tiriti o Waitangi and any proposed national
or any national direction, then Te Tiriti o Waitangi prevails.
It was inserted directly into our-- into the Waikato Regional Policy
Statement, by the government without the requirement to
progress through a Schedule One plan change process.
So it's imperative that the new legislation is crystal clear
how the existing Treaty settlement obligations in relation to Te Tiriti
o Waitangi will be met. Clause nine,
subclause one of the Natural Environment Bill references the equivalent
effect where the Crown will work with post-settlement
governance entities to seek agreement on how Treaty settlement
redress will operate with equivalency.
We recommend including recognition of Te Tiriti o Waitangi in
both bills to ensure its status is upheld in the new resource
management system, and we seek that Te Tiriti o Waitangi
is deemed to be part of the Regional Spatial Plan, the Natural
Environment Plan, and also the land use plans for the Waikato
region.
The next point I'd like to raise is to do with the Lake Taupō nutrient
management framework. This is referred to on page eleven--
sorry, paragraph eleven, page seven, submission, submission
point one sixty-seven on page sixty-two, and submission
point one sixty-nine on page
sixty-four. Taxpayers and
ratepayers have invested eighty-one billion dollars of public money
to improve water quality in the Lake Taupō catchment.
In addition, local government
and landowners have incurred significant additional
costs. The nitrogen cap and trade system
that was introduced was the first of its kind in the
world. It has successfully resulted in a
reduction of nitrogen entering the lake by purchasing at least twenty
percent of the nitrogen that was put onto land, or around a hundred and seventy
tons per, per hectare that was lost into the lake system.
And that was done using the eighty-one billion dollars of public money.
Simply, we cannot risk having this approach undone
inadvertently by the new system
or a transition to the new system. Careful consideration of the
implications of the bills on this existing nutrient capping planning framework is
needed, including ensuring that nationally prescribed approaches do not
undermine or limit the ability to continue to operate
established local catchment-specific
arrangements. Well-defined environment
limits exist for the catchment, and that are maintained through the use of these
innovative economic instruments, exactly what the system reform
is seeking to champion. So we ask that the new system
provides a mechanism for the current provisions to effectively be
lifted and shifted easily into the new legislation and
given effect to in the Natural Environment
Plan. The final point I'd like to
raise is of a more technical matter, and it's
to do with clauses thirty-two and one sixty-four of the
Natural Environment Bill or section seventy and one oh
seven, which are in the Resource Management Act, which have been taken over into
those new clauses. This relates to matters that have been brought to
our attention through the recent Environment Court process for a
significant plan change for the Waikato for land use.
That is Plan Change One. The court paused
making a decision while the Resource Management Act
recently was amended.
So to do with clause thirty-two or section seventy, and it relates to
submission point one forty-one on page forty-six of our submission.
So recently, the government amended the Resource Management Act at our
request to enable permitted activities under section
seventy. That is where some environmental
limits, cannot be met. This was
essential for the Environment Court to consider the validity of giving
permitted activity status for some low-risk farming activities
in Plan Change One. Without that
change, an additional two thousand eight hundred farmers would need
resource consent, probably for little benefit if
any.
Our submission simply recommends that clause thirty-two, the
principles for classifying activities of the Natural Environment
Bill, which is equivalent to section seventy of the RMA, should
reflect the amendments that were made to the section via the Consenting and
Other System Changes Amendment Act in August of last
year. Curiously, presently they do
not. As drafted, clause thirty-two
reverts back to the previous version of section seventy of the RMA.
This means that councils would require resource consents, from
landowners for activities where there's an environment, environmental
limit already being breached. Um, and that would be for either
permit-permitted activities or consents.
So if left as drafted, rules requiring some consents
or permits, I should say, in the new system, would need to be written for farming
activities that are presently permitted activities in our regional
plan. Now, looking at the companion
provision for the granting of those permits and consents.
So this is clause one sixty-four of the new bill or section one
oh seven of the current RMA, and it's referred to in submission point
two ten, on page
seventy-nine. While we support clause one
sixty-four, matters for which permit must not be
granted, we, we suggest that this clause should also
consider that where there's already a breach of a limit, the consenting authority
does have the ability to make a decision on an
application.Currently, Clause 164 states that
where an environment limit is breached, the consenting authority is unable to
grant a permit or consent. Our
suggested amendment to Clause 164C on page 79
provides for a consenting or s- a permitting pathway with the
necessary considerations. As stated in our submission
point, we consider that a permitting authority should be able to
grant a permit if another approach similar to RMA Section
1072A is adopted. Our sub- suggested
amendments at submission point 141 on page
46 and submission point 210 on page 79
would address these matters. Uh, finding it's
quite a technical matter, but just in an attempt to, to
simplify the, the conundrum I've just
described, I'll just describe it like this.
As presently written, when an activity is unable to be considered as a
permitted activity, as written in Clause 32, then a
consent's required. But where a consent is required,
one not, cannot be granted
because of the way that Clause 164 is written.
Something doesn't quite add up to us, and we believe it needs to be
corrected. The, it will have a
significant-- this will have a significant impact on activities undertaken in our
region, and as previously mentioned, at least 2,800
low-risk farming activities would need to obtain,
permits, which we cannot grant.
Our amendments seek for this, to, to,
provide a c- a pathway in both instances.
Pathways that allow the options of assessing the current breach of the environment
limit and so that everybody can be satisfied that conditions can be
applied that will contribute to remedying the environment limit breach
over time. Th- th- th-
Pass that to Ryan to finish.
Uh, well, I'm, I'm happy not to wind up right now.
Um, I see Ryan's got his hand up for questions, and I'd rather, I'd rather w-we
got the questions.
Yeah. That'd be great.
So I think that's all right.
Um, and thank you. Very good submission. Thanks. Some great points.
I'll let the, the member of Parliament for the fastest growing city in the country
have a say.
Thank, thanks, Chair. Hey, thanks, team, for your presentation.
And, yeah, obviously a lot of similar themes coming in from all over the
country. Um, couple of quick questions for you guys.
In your submission-
Mm-hmm
... you talk about, data sets and,
you mention significant, gaps in environmental
data are needed to set limits. So I guess my question is what, what data
sets or monitoring capabilities are most lacking, and what would it,
what would it help to sort of fill that for you guys from a,
compliance or a, a council
perspective?
You want that, Christine?
Yeah. Um, thanks, Ryan. I guess for us, the,
some of those data sets are really just consistency of data across the
entire region. One that springs to mind is probably groundwater.
That's an area of, focus or
for gaps, identifying gaps. And also, as you'll be
aware, a third of our region is in fact the coastal marine
area, and, and it's a bit of a poor cousin to,
some of the data that we have on land and freshwater.
Okay. Thanks for that. And one more question if I could, Chair.
Yep, fire away. Last one. Yep.
Um, where'd it go? Oh, just on gaps between the two
bills. You've identified gaps, and you'd like us to
consider effects on wetlands, geothermal features, outstanding natural
landscapes. I understand some of this might come through the policy direction,
in next, next statements, but were there any other conflicting or
gaps you think we need to be aware of between the two bills?
Um, me again, Ryan. Um, I think the, the key is making
sure under the planning bill where we are address, addressing
kind of land use activities, that we are also making sure some of the
environmental limits are taken account of.
Um, and one that springs to mind, particularly with
respect to residential and commercial activity, is freshwater
and that link to water allocation and, water
quantity.
Yeah. Okay. Thank you.
Uh, look, thank you very much. We've reached our, allotted time.
Really appreciate your submission. Very, very helpful.
And, thank you very much. Have a great day.
Well, hey, thanks, Ryan.
And, and more than happy to make any of our staff available to, your
officials, that are advising you in the select committee.
Yeah. The, the technical points you pointed out, they're very helpful.
Thank you very much. Yeah.
Thank you.
Thank you.
All the best.
Okay. Cheers. Right. We should have our
next, Te Topuni
Kokorangi. Are they up next?
Who... Here we go. Is that Sean I see
there?
Hi.
Kia ora. Good morning.
Is it on?
Uh, tena koutou. Um-
Yeah. Good morning. Uh, hi. Good morning.
Uh, Grant McMillan, I'm the chair of the subcommittee.
Uh, we have 15 minutes with you. Um,
we, you take your submission as read, but please feel free to highlight a few
key points, and away we go. Thank you.
Welcome.
Right. E oku rangatira, tena koutou katoa.
Uh, ko Liana Poutu toku ingoa. Ko au te, pou
whakarae, te hiamana me kī o Te Topuni Kokorangi.
Kei konei, toku ringaringa, Sean
Zilches. Uh, e mihi ana ki a koutou tena ata.
Uh, good morning, everybody. My name is Liana Poutu.
Uh, I, along with Sean Zilches, are here presenting on
behalf of Te Kāhui Tupua, which is the legal personality for
Taranaki Maunga and the other peaks.
Uh, the entity that we represent, is Te
Topuni Kokorangi, so I chair that entity.
Um, and also, Sean is our technical
planner for Te Topuni Kokorangi. Uh, as you said, we're not gonna
read through our submission. We will, will highlight a few
things. I guess the, the first thing I want to,
um-Probably kick off with is in our settlement
legislation, Te Türe Whakatupua mo te Kahui
Tupua. Uh, Te Tupane Kookorangi has legal
standing to participate in a range
of, of fora, to represent Te
Kahui Tupua. One of our, our key
concerns, I guess the concern that underlies,
our position on, on these bills is
that we have, legal standing to
participate. Uh, that's-- that is also extended
outside of the boundaries of the national
park. So although the legal personality,
is described as being the legal personality for the
peaks, we also have standing to, operate
in fora where the impacts
are, are greater than, than the ring plane, what we refer to as the ring
plane of, of the national park of Te Papakura o
Taranaki. One of the, the concerns we have
with the bill is the way that Te Kahui Tupua
as a legal personality has been included in the, the broad
definition of,
of Maori land, interested Maori land.
And, and one of the reasons for that is because it doesn't
fully, prescribe and define what
the extent of Te Kahui Tupua is. So I don't think we're even
named currently in the bill. Uh, there's a reference there to Te
Urwera, which is one of the other legal personalities,
over a natural feature. And so that definition
really, contains and
seems to restrict, what our settlement
legislation actually provides for Te Kahui Tupua and
us as the representative face and, voice
entity. So I guess it's, it's with that that our,
I guess our submissions are really, are based around the
fact that these bills aim to
or-- they, they seem to restrict,
what we already have in our settlement legislation.
So I'm gonna hand now to Sean, who's gonna talk through, really just
highlight for you some of those technical issues, and then, and then I'll close us
off at the end. So kia ora, Sean.
Tena tatou. Uh, yeah, as Leanne has referred to
the, I guess, the reference to Te Kahui Tupua is relatively
clumsy in the bills at the moment. It, it's shoehorned in to identify Maori
land, and then the provisions of both bills that hang from that
definition, dramatically
constrain and I guess look to redefine what Te
Kahui Tupua is, which, which rubs up against,
Te Tiriti o Kupe Takirauano and, and the legislation that implements
that. Um, that has quite a significant practical effect for
Te Kahui Tupua, both in terms of how
it, can implement its standing in resource management matters,
and then secondly, how, how difficult it,
how difficult it will be to implement some of those within the transition
time frames that are, that are set in the legislation.
There's a real challenge of, trying to renegotiate
or re- recast some of these
arra- some of the settlement arrangements or the arrangements for Te Kahui
Tupua in a two-year window concurrent to national planning
frameworks, regional spatial plans, local area
plans all starting to, to, to be developed and hit the
road. And for a legal person such as Te Kahui Tupua,
which sits broadly across the Taranaki region, that, that's quite a
fundamental and substantial impact that, that as, as
Leanne has mentioned, starts to rub up against what the, what Te Tiriti o
Kupe Takirauano, sets out. Um,
the, the, the redress and, and Te Kahui Tupua have a
set of values, Ngā Pu Vakatupua, and Te Kahui Tupua status,
which, which, to be, have,
have, the ability to be considered
and, and influence any activity that may impact on Te Kahui
Tupua. So I think the-- I guess the rub is
pretty, pretty evident in that when the scope of
planning, plan making, the scope of any resource
consent or equivalent type, processes is, is narrowed
dramatically,
even more so when it's shoehorned into the definition of identified
Maori land. Um, look, turning, turning
to, I guess, the plan making processes that, that sit in both
bills, there's no standing
for Te Kahui Tupua. Um, it's another
discretionary interest to be weighed.
It would need to submit alongside everybody else,
to, to, to, I guess, to provide that advice and provide that
input into any plan making, which starts to push up against
the, the, the express, provisions of the, the settlement
legislation fifty-three two. Um, so
look, in our submission, we've provided some, some drafting.
It's-- She's pretty fast drafting.
So I think that, it's more the,
it's more the principles and ideas than that drafting more so than, than
the wording specifically that we're wedded to.
But those really just seek to, insert into the
process the, the, the recognition of standing and the role of Ngāpu
Vakatupua and Te Kahui Tupua within, within, within the Taranaki
region. But I'll, I'll s- I'll stop there 'cause that's sort of
the fundamental issues. There's a range of other kind of bits that flow from
that and, and the intricacies of, of both bills, but, but the-- it
all starts from, I guess, the way this-- these bills are cast
and the way that they, um-
The way they understand Te Kāhui Tupua.
Right. So sections nine and ten of,
the Natural Environments Bill as it is are what we're referring
to as the equivalency, equivalency
bills, pa- provisions, sorry.
And so
what, what those do for us is say that while
there is a negotiation to be had between the pa- between the Crown
and treaty settlement partners, to determine how
best to recognize and uphold treaty settlements,
that to the greatest extent possible, that will
be, you know, the existing,
the existing arrangements will be,
applied. Uh, and certainly for that, that two-year
period that's prescribed. Our view is it took us nine
years to negotiate these arrangements, and I'm not hopeful
that a two-year, period in which to
resolve a number of, of issues is
appropriate. And we were one-- we were only one treaty,
treaty
redress, arr- set of arrangements.
I think the, the other point is greatest
extent possible gives us less than what we currently have
legislatively. And so there are things that will need to be
resolved. I don't believe that, two years will
be enough. And once that, I think it's nine three that
repeals the section after two years from a-
from, enforcement date,
we're sort of left in a, in a situation where the
Crown can't usu- can't unilaterally amend treaty
settlements without the agreement of the treaty partner.
Uh, and so we run ourselves into a
point where actually we're going to be kind of
deadlocked
potentially because we just don't have and haven't
had the time to work through what these arrangements mean going
forward. So I think we'll end it there so that we can, enable
questions from the committee. Tēnā koutou.
Thank you very much. Um, Huhana, I believe, has a question for
you.
Tēnā koe, Leana. O tira, tēnā koe, Shaun, mo
tō wāhi kōrero i tēnei ata. Um, wanting to
really talk with you around,
engagement and, just the way
that
our Kāhui Tupua has been treated,
in the preparation and drafting of this legislation.
We've heard that it's not even listed within
the bills. Um, was there any engagement from
Crown officials with you, in
preparation? And what was the quality of that engagement if
you were i- included in
conversations?
So no, no engagement specifically with Te Kāhui Tupua, with
Te Tupahi Kōkōrangi. Uh, in fairness, we
are relatively new
entity. Our, our settlement legislation went through at the beginning of last
year. Um, however, we've been in
negotiation for nine years, so, you know, I don't use
it. That's not an excuse for not knowing that these arrangements were,
were coming. So it's-- we've had none.
We've had no engagement, no input.
Uh, and, and
I believe that this has been done back to front.
I thought you would resolve, how treaty settlements
will move forward in the new planning space prior
to introducing bills that have holding clauses
essentially to say, "We're gonna give you two years
to renegotiate and re-agree every
treaty settlement that's, you know, occurred in the country." I, I
think that's unrealistic,
yeah, and, and, and unfair on the parties to have to do that in that
timeframe.
Mr. Chair, a supplementary on that one.
Yeah. Yeah, go on.
So, you know,
you, you, you are a known entity. Uh, y- the, the
settlement came through the house.
We were there and, provided speeches of support.
So you weren't included or even considered.
We, we see that in the legislation.
Uh, and yet, this approach was meant to be a new
way of environmental governance, of that mahi tahi,
in looking after, you know, the kāhui maunga as we have with the
Ngāhere and Niuerevera and Te Awatapua.
So,
i- in my mind, why should you need to
relitigate a treaty settlement that the ink has barely
dried, for this legislation? Because really
you're a whakauruho,
and, and what has been agreed should be upheld
by any government, in environmental reform.
Because really you're already providing the framework as to how the mahi tahi is
meant to occur. Is that correct?
Agree one hundred percent. Agree one hundred percent.
And, and in fact, you know, Taranaki Maunga and Te
Papakura o Taranaki, the national park,
provides all of the sustenance to our region.
It provides all of the you know, the water to our region.
Um, it's a key component to, to
how, environmental,
planning, you know, done in this region.
Taranaki Maunga is a key component, and at present,
Taranaki Maunga is excluded from having a role in helping
develop those.
Kia ora.
Yeah. Well, thank you very lot. We've reached our time.
So look, thank you very much for your, contribution this morning.
Really appreciate it. And, we'll, yeah, we'll-- and, yeah, have a great
day. So thank you. Kia ora.
Tēnā koutou. Ngā mihi.
Right, so we'll now bring up our next
submitter.
Next submitter, Ngā Hapū o Ngāti
Porou. Here we go. Kia ora.
Tena koe.
Good morning. Ah, yes. Ah, yes, we, we, we meet again.
Good morning. Kia ora. Uh, hi, Grant McCallum here.
Um, welcome to the, subcommittee, of the
Environment Select Committee this morning.
I'm chairing this one, and we've got-- you've got 15 minutes of our time.
Please take your submission as read, but you're welcome to highlight a few points,
and then we'll leave time for questions. It's all yours. Thank you.
Ka pai, tena tatou. I'll just check you can hear me okay.
Perfect. Thank you.
Excellent. Um, well, morning everybody.
Um, I appear for Ngā Rohe Moana o Ngāti Porou alongside my
whanaunga, Matanuku Mahuika, and Agnes Walker, who you can see on screen.
My name's Huriana Uenis Tope. I'm here in a legal capacity.
I'm gonna hand it over to Matanuku to start the submission this morning, and
then, he'll pass back over to me for some technical points, and
then we'll leave time for questions. So I'll hand over to you, Ma.
Tena koutou. Um, tena koe, e te hiamona,
and members of the committee. Um,
I'll be relatively quick. I'm, I'm here, I was the
legal advisor through the majority of this process through to
2015 when my father, who was the chief negotiator,
passed away,
and I became the, the negotiator at that point through to the passing
of the bespoke Ngāti Porou legislation in
2019. Um, the, the context
of this is that, this was a,
a bespoke agreement that, well, negotiations for
which began in the end of 2003, start of
2004, following, was then Labor
government's
announcing its intention to legislate to prevent, Māori
claiming customary title in relation to the foreshore and seabed.
Um, and the, the challenge for us through this negotiation was
balancing both the, the politics of the foreshore and
seabed, but, but
also the-- our view at the time, and it remains our
view, was that this was a national response, but it failed to take
into account our very specific circumstances.
Um, and we've discussed those circumstances previously.
By far the majority population living in the Tairāwhiti is Ngāti
Porou. Um, we've retained the vast
majority of our coastal lands. We maintain a very,
strong and ongoing connection to the, the taku tai.
We don't wanna keep the public out, but we do want to make sure that our
interests are protected. So the challenge that we had was: How do
we, how do we ensure that we,
are able to exert, a level of influence which is
commensurate with the strength of our connection,
but we do so in a, in a way which navigates around the various,
statutory instruments? A-and the context of the RMA, the RMA is different
in the marine environment,
and it's different because on dry land you can identify a
landowner. It deals with environmental impacts but doesn't allocate
resources. In the marine environment, it has an allocative ex-
effect. It allocates space. So it's necessary to interact
with that regime in order to, prevent it from
undermining, you know, the rights that we say that we
have and that were recognized through this agreement.
So the, the short point is that, you know, we worked our way through,
ensuring that we had effective
oversight and input, into the management of the taku
tai moana which, at the most extreme ends, where we have the strongest interests,
we can prevent an activity from occurring, accepting
ongoing public access, and also accepting certain things of national
significance. So it was a very carefully crafted
compromise. An amendment to the RMA as being proposed
will affect that compromise
a-and will effectively be a, you know, a
repositioning of us a-and will require
